<|user|>
How do I harden SSH on my Debian server?
<|assistant|>
Edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`:

```
# Disable root login
PermitRootLogin no

# Disable password auth (use keys only)
PasswordAuthentication no

# Use only SSH protocol 2
Protocol 2

# Limit to specific users
AllowUsers deploy admin

# Change default port (optional)
Port 2222

# Idle timeout
ClientAliveInterval 300
ClientAliveCountMax 2
```

Restart: `sudo systemctl restart sshd`

**Important**: Make sure you have key-based access working before disabling password auth, or you'll lock yourself out.

<|user|>
How do I set up SSH key authentication?
<|assistant|>
On your local machine:
```bash
# Generate key pair
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@email"

# Copy public key to server
ssh-copy-id user@server

# Or manually
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub | ssh user@server 'mkdir -p ~/.ssh && cat >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys'
```

On the server, ensure permissions:
```bash
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
```

ed25519 is preferred over RSA — shorter keys, faster, more secure.

<|user|>
How do I set up fail2ban?
<|assistant|>
```bash
sudo apt install fail2ban
```

Create `/etc/fail2ban/jail.local`:
```ini
[DEFAULT]
bantime = 1h
findtime = 10m
maxretry = 5

[sshd]
enabled = true
port = ssh
filter = sshd
logpath = /var/log/auth.log
maxretry = 3
```

Start it:
```bash
sudo systemctl enable --now fail2ban
```

Check status:
```bash
sudo fail2ban-client status sshd
```

Unban an IP: `sudo fail2ban-client set sshd unbanip 192.168.1.50`

<|user|>
How do I manage file permissions and ownership?
<|assistant|>
```bash
# Change owner
chown user:group file
chown -R user:group directory/

# Change permissions
chmod 644 file      # rw-r--r--
chmod 755 directory  # rwxr-xr-x
chmod u+x script.sh  # add execute for owner

# Octal: owner-group-other
# 7=rwx, 6=rw-, 5=r-x, 4=r--, 0=---

# Special permissions
chmod u+s binary     # setuid — run as owner
chmod g+s directory   # setgid — new files inherit group
chmod +t /tmp         # sticky bit — only owner can delete
```

Use `ls -la` to view permissions. The sticky bit on /tmp is why users can't delete each other's temp files.

<|user|>
How do I configure sudo access?
<|assistant|>
Use `visudo` to safely edit `/etc/sudoers`:
```bash
sudo visudo
```

Common patterns:
```
# Full sudo access
username ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL

# Sudo without password
username ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL

# Specific commands only
username ALL=(ALL) /usr/bin/systemctl restart nginx, /usr/bin/apt update

# Group-based (preferred)
%admin ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL
```

Better practice: add files in `/etc/sudoers.d/` instead of editing the main file:
```bash
echo 'username ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/systemctl restart nginx' | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/username
sudo chmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/username
```

<|user|>
How do I check for rootkits?
<|assistant|>
Two main tools:

```bash
# rkhunter
sudo apt install rkhunter
sudo rkhunter --update
sudo rkhunter --check

# chkrootkit
sudo apt install chkrootkit
sudo chkrootkit
```

Also useful:
```bash
# Check for unusual SUID binaries
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null

# Check listening services
ss -tlnp

# Check running processes
ps auxf

# Check for unusual cron jobs
for user in $(cut -f1 -d: /etc/passwd); do crontab -l -u $user 2>/dev/null; done
```

Prevention is better than detection. Keep packages updated: `sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade`

<|user|>
How do I set up automatic security updates on Debian?
<|assistant|>
```bash
sudo apt install unattended-upgrades
sudo dpkg-reconfigure -plow unattended-upgrades
```

This enables automatic installation of security updates. The config is in `/etc/apt/apt.conf.d/50unattended-upgrades`.

Key settings:
```
Unattended-Upgrade::Allowed-Origins {
    "${distro_id}:${distro_codename}-security";
};
Unattended-Upgrade::Automatic-Reboot "false";
Unattended-Upgrade::Mail "admin@example.com";
```

Check logs: `cat /var/log/unattended-upgrades/unattended-upgrades.log`

<|user|>
How do I use AppArmor on Debian?
<|assistant|>
AppArmor is installed and enabled by default on Debian:

```bash
# Check status
sudo aa-status

# List profiles
sudo aa-status --profiles

# Set a profile to complain mode (log but don't block)
sudo aa-complain /usr/sbin/nginx

# Set to enforce mode
sudo aa-enforce /usr/sbin/nginx

# Disable a profile
sudo ln -s /etc/apparmor.d/usr.sbin.nginx /etc/apparmor.d/disable/
sudo apparmor_parser -R /etc/apparmor.d/usr.sbin.nginx
```

Profiles live in `/etc/apparmor.d/`. Use `aa-genprof` to generate a new profile interactively.
